labrador retriever.breed_note: Labrador Retrievers are prone to hip and elbow dysplasia. They have a strong tendency toward obesity, which worsens joint problems. Exercise-induced collapse can occur in young, active dogs.

golden retriever.breed_note: Golden Retrievers have elevated cancer rates compared to other breeds. Hip dysplasia and skin/coat allergies are common. Regular cardiac screening is recommended.

german shepherd.breed_note: German Shepherds are highly susceptible to hip dysplasia and degenerative myelopathy (progressive spinal cord disease). Bloat (GDV) is a life-threatening risk, especially after meals.

bulldog.breed_note: Bulldogs have Brachycephalic Obstructive Airway Syndrome (BOAS) due to their flat faces, causing breathing difficulties. Skin fold dermatitis requires regular cleaning. Overheating is a serious risk.

french bulldog.breed_note: French Bulldogs are prone to breathing difficulties, spinal issues (IVDD), and skin allergies. They must not be left in hot environments and cannot swim due to their body shape.

poodle.breed_note: Poodles are predisposed to Addison's disease (adrenal gland disorder) and bloat. Progressive retinal atrophy can lead to blindness. Generally long-lived with proper care.

beagle.breed_note: Beagles are highly prone to obesity and are food-motivated, requiring portion control. Epilepsy affects a significant portion of the breed. Hypothyroidism is also common.

rottweiler.breed_note: Rottweilers have elevated risk for bone cancer (osteosarcoma) and aortic stenosis (heart defect). Hip dysplasia is very common. Bloat is a serious risk.

yorkshire terrier.breed_note: Yorkshire Terriers are prone to patellar luxation (kneecap dislocation) and liver shunts. Tracheal collapse can cause chronic coughing. Small breeds like Yorkies can experience dangerous blood sugar drops.

boxer.breed_note: Boxers have one of the highest cancer rates among dog breeds. Arrhythmogenic Right Ventricular Cardiomyopathy (ARVC) is breed-specific. Breathing issues from their brachycephalic structure.

dachshund.breed_note: Dachshunds' long spine makes them highly susceptible to intervertebral disc disease (IVDD), which can cause paralysis. Obesity dramatically increases this risk. Avoid stairs and jumping.

siberian husky.breed_note: Siberian Huskies are prone to progressive retinal atrophy leading to blindness. They can develop zinc-responsive dermatosis. Generally hardy but sensitive to heat.

persian cat.breed_note: Persian cats have high rates of polycystic kidney disease (PKD). Their flat face causes breathing difficulties and dental crowding. Facial folds require daily cleaning.

maine coon.breed_note: Maine Coons are predisposed to hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM), the most common heart disease in cats. Spinal muscular atrophy can cause muscle weakness.

siamese cat.breed_note: Siamese cats are prone to respiratory infections. They have higher rates of liver amyloidosis and dental issues due to their narrow jaw. Crossed eyes and kinked tails are breed traits.

british shorthair.breed_note: British Shorthairs have significant risk of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and PKD. Their calm nature and love of food make obesity a real concern requiring portion management.
